content.text: Worksheets, not licenses. Every step points at a page that already owns the number. If a step seems to need a number nobody prints, you found a design gap — file it, don't fill it.

Four worksheets: a technique, a profession, a wild graft, an enemy. None of them contains a new rule. Each is the printed framework restated as a checklist, with the budget constraints from Power Budget & Price List wired in at the step where they bite.

content.label: THE ONE-PLACE LAW
content.text: Every number lives on exactly one page; every other page references it by name. Homebrew obeys the same law: your new entry references the owning page for every value it leans on, and the only new text anywhere is the entry itself. Quick-references are generated from source tables, never hand-copied — hand-copying is what killed the old book.
The Ownership Map
Before any worksheet: this is where the numbers live. Reference these pages; never restate them.

rows: The currency price list, stack caps, the three laws, ceilings, the lane law, the seven-point checklist
Tier chassis, unlock counts, install costs, the density standard
Technique rungs (cost, downtime), training sources, the technique ceiling
PP economy, profession tiers, perk price band, income by rank
Splice/graft procedure, Risk DCs, provider modifiers, the Saturation floor, the five-line graft worksheet
Enemy tier chassis, body picks, role modifiers, Break lines, the profile card, horde compression
Carve yields by taxonomy, the preservation clock
Weapons (currently a flagged gap — see Power Budget & Price List)

content.text: Worksheet 1 — Building a Technique
A technique is a trained move any contractor can buy — squad craft, weapon handling, body mechanics. The catalog, the rung ladder, and the ceiling live on Techniques.

Name the fundamental. Lane check first: a technique is never damage and never a tree's identity verb. If the move is a body verb, it's an augment; if it's bare competence, it's a skill feature. (The lane law — Power Budget & Price List.)

Price the effect. On the price list, a technique may carry at most +1 of any capped currency, conditional only — that is Techniques' printed ceiling, deliberately below what augments may buy. Effects outside the capped currencies (a quick-action discount, a movement rider) pattern-match against the printed catalog.

Pick the rung — Trained, Advanced, or Master. Costs and downtime live on Techniques. Calibrate by comparison: set your move next to the printed moves at that rung and ask which list it joins without embarrassment.

Cost the action. Passive, or active with an AP cost. If it acts during someone else's turn, it is an interrupt and costs RP — every interrupt gets an RP cost, no exceptions (RP repricing — Power Budget & Price List).

Run the audits. Scar law. Beat budget if it touches crits — spend beats, never build a parallel crit system. Duplicate kill-list: if a tree, skill, profession, or mod already does this, the effect already has a home; reference it or drop yours.

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: Technique prerequisites are "tier-based" but never specified, and the training-source table carries pre-rebuild tier labels — both flagged on Techniques. Homebrew techniques inherit those holes; there is no prerequisite framework to comply with yet.
Worksheet 2 — Building a Profession
A profession is position in the economy, not competence. The chassis — PP sources, tiers, perk pricing, income — lives on Professions Overview.

Name what it owns. One economic position, stated as ownership: the Fixer owns who, the Guide owns where, the Carver owns what the kill is worth. A profession that owns nothing is a skill wearing a coat.

Write the two verbs. A Work verb — downtime labor that pays credits and PP at the rates Professions Overview prints — and a field verb, the on-mission exercise of the trade, earning PP at the printed per-mission cap. A profession whose field verb never fires levels only at home; Carvers, Wranglers, and Guides level in the field by design.

Write the perks. Priced inside the printed PP band on Professions Overview, entry perk first; tiers unlock by perks owned, per the chassis there. A few perks may also cost credits — some kit is money and expertise at once.

Lane audit. Perks grant position — clients, licenses, access, scale — never skill dice (skill dice live on The Twelve Skills). Where a perk touches a combat currency, it prices on the price list like everything else, shared caps and all.

Portability audit — the tattoo-artist law. The name travels; premises don't exist. If a perk needs walls, the walls belong to the squad's base (see The Base), and the base amplifies the profession — the perk is never made of the building.

Obligations and the grave. Established and Name perks may carry an Obligation — a standing expectation that is part of the perk's price; the cost of being known is being known. And everything dies with the person: no perk may transfer rank, clients, or banked PP.

Scale check. Capstoning a profession costs what peak human costs — the PP arithmetic is printed on Professions Overview, and The Anchor Table puts every flagship investment on one scale. A profession that capstones for a fraction of that calendar is off the scale and underpriced.
Worksheet 3 — Building a Wild Graft

The five-line worksheet is printed canon on Mutations & Grafts, where it opens at the Frontier dial setting. This is the same procedure with the budget hooks called out. Build a graft from any creature, in five lines:
Donor capability. Name the one thing the creature does that the organ carries. The creature's signature line is the menu — profile cards live in the Bestiary.

Price the benefit on the price list. Caps apply, shared with everything else you wear and install. Capped currencies arrive as +1 conditional — the way mods price them (see Mods). The worked example on Mutations & Grafts prices a Char Hound heart at +1 RP, sharing the RP cap, audited.
Write the mandatory cost. One mechanical drawback in the same domain — the organ wants what the animal wanted: appetite, light, heat, motion. The cost is part of the price, not flavor.

Set Risk and scope. Risk tier = the creature's tier; the Risk DCs, provider modifiers, and the surgery's Fate Roll live on Mutations & Grafts. Systemic — marrow, glands feeding the whole body — means the bloodline fingerprint line comes with it.
Write the scanner line. What a dome checkpoint sees. Every entry has one; a mutation is a paperwork problem before it is anything else.

Standing costs the worksheet never waives: every mutation raises the Saturation floor by the amount Mutations & Grafts prints, and the donor organ runs on the preservation clock from Carving — price the benefit, then check whether the organ is still worth installing by the time it reaches a table.

content.label: THE SCAR LAW'S COUSIN
content.text: Nothing a graft buys may touch the Fate Roll's death math. The body you build can make you strange — never safe.
Worksheet 4 — Statting an Enemy
Four picks, two minutes: Tier → Body → Role → 1–2 abilities. Every framework number in the picks lives on Enemy Framework; your block prints the result.
Tier. Fodder, Standard, or Elite — take HP, accuracy, damage, Evasion, and RP straight from the chassis table on Enemy Framework. Bosses are sized, not statted; that procedure lives on Bosses.

Body. Flesh, Hide, or Shell. The pick decides which damage math runs, which weapons counter it, and what the corpse is worth. The per-body guidance — fauna HP, Hide values, who wears the player armor ladder — is printed on Enemy Framework.

Role. Apply one role's chassis modifier as printed on Enemy Framework. The role also hands the enemy its 1–2 abilities — pattern-match against the Bestiary's signature lines, and remember the framework's blanket claim: the price list governs all catalogs, including this one. An ability that hands an enemy a currency no player tier could buy needs a reason.

Break line. Every block carries one — the condition where its survival math flips. The Break procedure and its check live on Enemy Framework; fodder just breaks, constructs have no line. Fear is arithmetic; write the line, not a morale subsystem.

Print the profile card. Five lines: Stat · Signature · Break · Carve · Graft. Carve goes by body taxonomy and prices on Carving; the Graft line is what Worksheet 3 consumes. Every entry feeds the economy — the party kills it, the Carver prices it.
Budget the encounter, not the body. Encounters are budgeted in activations (Enemy Framework); crowds compress into hordes rather than drowning the clock.

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: Two inherited holes, both flagged on Enemy Framework: no per-role ability menu is printed, and no activation budget exists below bosses. A homebrew enemy can be statted to the chassis today; the menu it should draw from and the budget it should be audited against are not yet written.

Whatever you build, ship it the way the catalogs shipped: against the seven-point checklist on Power Budget & Price List, sim gate included. The framework prices effects; the checklist catches the ones that lie about their price.
